1:
After the secular tribes—Judah with its kings, Simeon, Reuben, Gad, and the half of Manasseh—the Chronicler comes at last to the tribe he most cares for, and the reformed John Gill catches the design in a sentence: the kingdom was given to Judah, the birthright to Joseph, and the priesthood to Levi, and it is the priestly tribe whose posterity the historian now unfolds. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke observes that the genealogy of Levi is set down here more ample and correct than that of any other tribe, and finds in this a further proof that the author was himself a priest, who felt for the priesthood and drew from the most accurate tables he possessed.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch lay out the whole chapter in advance, for it falls into distinct registers: the high-priestly line of Aaron down to Jehozadak, whom Nebuchadnezzar carried into exile (v.15); the shorter lists of the families of Gershon, Kohath, and Merari; the genealogies of the singers Heman, Asaph, and Ethan, with notes on the service of the other Levites; and finally the register of the high priests from Eleazar to Ahimaaz, with the cities given the Levites. They notice too a small unevenness that betrays the compiler's sources: the eldest son of Levi stands as Gershon at the head of the chapter and as Gershom below (v.16), one man under two forms of his name.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry draws the pastoral point that makes such a list worth keeping. The priests and Levites were more concerned than any other Israelites to preserve their pedigree clear and to be able to prove it, because all the honours and privileges of their office hung upon their descent; and he recalls those who, after the captivity, could not find the register of their genealogy and were therefore, as polluted, put from the priesthood and forbidden to eat of the holy things (Ezra 2:62-63). Yet Henry lifts the eye higher: now the Spirit of God calls ministers to their work without limit of the family they spring from, and though believers and ministers may be very useful to the church, none but our great High Priest can make atonement for sin, nor can any be accepted but through his atonement. The witnesses thus open the chapter with one accord—a tribe set apart, a pedigree jealously kept, and a priesthood that for all its dignity could make nothing perfect, but waited for a better hope.
2:
With the sons of Kohath—Amram, Izhar, Hebron, and Uzziel—the register bends at once toward its true centre, for it is through Kohath that Aaron and the high priests come. The reformed John Gill notes only that the four sons stand in the same order as in the older books, content to let the law supply what the Chronicler abridges. It is the grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch who open the deeper purpose of the list that follows.

From Amram came Aaron and Moses and Miriam, and from Aaron, Nadab and Abihu, Eleazar and Ithamar; and then, through Eleazar and Phinehas and Abishua, the historian counts the descendants down twenty-two generations, the word “he begat” repeated at every name. Keil and Delitzsch draw from the older commentators the right inference: the son so begotten, living after his father, was heir to the high-priestly dignity, so that the whole enumeration is in truth a succession of high priests, from Eleazar who died shortly after Joshua (Josh. 24:33) to Jehozadak whom Nebuchadnezzar carried to Babylon. Phinehas the son of Eleazar, they observe, is already found in possession of the office in the days of the judges (Judg. 20:28), the same Phinehas whose zeal the law records (Exod. 6:25).

They add the chronology that governs the list. From the death of Aaron in the fortieth year after Israel came out of Egypt to the building of the temple in the fourth year of Solomon, some four hundred and forty years elapsed, and the generations named must be fitted to that span. The witnesses do not divide here; Gill and Keil and Delitzsch alike read the sons of Kohath not as a bare tally of names but as the doorway into the sacred succession, the channel through which the priesthood ran unbroken from the wilderness to the exile. What looks like the driest of lists is, in their hands, the spine of Israel's worship, each begetting a link that carried the altar's service on to the next generation until the captivity broke the line off, and it waited to be taken up again in Jehozadak's son Joshua after the return.
3:
Three names of surpassing weight are gathered into this single verse—Aaron, Moses, and Miriam—with the four sons of Aaron set beside them. The reformed John Gill marks them as very memorable persons: Aaron the high priest, Moses the prophet and lawgiver, and Miriam the prophetess, the three through whom God led and taught his people. The list keeps them here not for narrative but for descent, yet the witnesses cannot let such names pass as mere links in a chain.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry lingers over them with reverence. This was that Moses and Aaron whom God honoured in making them the instruments of Israel's deliverance and settlement, and figures of him that was to come—Moses as a prophet, Aaron as a priest. And though Nadab and Abihu left no children, and so had no place to claim in a genealogy, Henry will not let their names go by without remembering the terrors of that divine justice they were made monuments of, when they offered strange fire and were consumed, that we may always fear before him. The verse that simply records a succession thus carries, for Henry, both the glory of the priesthood's founders and a warning written into the very household of Aaron.

The sons of Aaron are named in their order—Nadab and Abihu, then Eleazar and Ithamar—and the witnesses know already which of the four will carry the line. Nadab and Abihu are struck from the story by their own presumption; the priesthood will run through Eleazar, with Ithamar's house holding it for a season in the time of the judges. So the verse stands as a hinge: it names the great generation that brought Israel out of Egypt and gave it law and altar, and at the same stroke it names the two sons whose fate shadows every priest who would come after, that the nearness of these men to God was no license but a summons to holy fear. Gill and Henry together make of the bare roll a meditation, the one fixing who these persons were, the other pressing home what their memory should work in those who read it.
16:
The genealogy doubles back on itself, naming the sons of Levi—Gershom, Kohath, and Merari—a second time, and the witnesses explain why the historian repeats himself. The reformed John Gill says plainly that the three are set down again for the sake of their posterity, whose names are given in the verses that follow; the first naming served the high-priestly line of Aaron, this second opens the wider families of the tribe. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry had already noted this doubling—the first fathers of the tribe named twice—and found in it no confusion but the method of a careful register, which lists the heads afresh before tracing each of their branches.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch set out the plan of this whole division of the chapter. It is introduced by an enumeration of the sons and grandsons of Levi, and then unfolds in six lines of descent: the families of Gershon, of Kohath, and of Merari, and after them the genealogies of David's chief musicians—Heman the Kohathite, Asaph the Gershonite, and Ethan the Merarite—closing with notes on the service of the other Levites and the priests. Each name in these lists, they observe, is joined to the one before by the little word “his son,” so that the reader is carried down generation by generation; but the lists differ greatly in length, some running far and some cut short.

Keil and Delitzsch mark here the same slight unevenness they noticed at the chapter's head: the eldest son, written Gershon in the first verse, appears from this point on as Gershom, the same man under a fuller form of his name. It is a token, they judge, that the compiler worked from more than one document, keeping each as he found it rather than smoothing the forms into agreement. The witnesses converge on the sense of the repetition: it is not a stammer in the text but a fresh start, the trunk named once more so that the three great boughs of Levi—Gershom, Kohath, and Merari—may each be followed in turn, and the whole tribe, singers and servants and priests alike, be shown springing from one root.
20:
The line of Gershom is paid out first, through his elder son Libni, and the witnesses attend to the string of names and the puzzle at its end. The reformed John Gill traces the descent—Jahath, Zimmah, Joah, Iddo, Zerah, Jeaterai—pausing to note that several of them bore second names elsewhere: Jahath is the Jehiel of another list, Joah the same as Ethan, Iddo called Adaiah, and Jeaterai known also as Ethni. He observes that the posterity of Shimei, Libni's brother, is here passed over, only the one branch being carried down.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read the little particle before Gershom's name as introductory—“as to Gershom”—and confirm that the descendants enumerated belong to Libni, the eldest son, traced through seven generations to Jeaterai, a name met with nowhere else. Of the intermediate names, they note that Jahath, Zimmah, and Zerah recur among the descendants of Asaph, who springs from the other Gershonite line of Shimei; the same names reappearing in the two branches. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott agrees, pointing back to the law's notice of “the clan of the Libnite and the clan of the Shimeite” as the clans of the Gershonite (Num. 3:21), and marking the same three recurring names, Jahath, Zimmah, and Zerah, in Asaph's pedigree below.

Over Jeaterai, in whom the series culminates, Ellicott ventures a small emendation. The name is wholly unknown, yet it has the ending of a patronymic or clan-name; and he suggests it should perhaps be pointed differently and read “Ithrai” or “the Ithrite,” a clan from which came two of David's heroes (1 Chron. 11:40). At the time the list was first drawn, he thinks, the name may have stood for a famous chieftain or family since faded from memory; he even compares the shift of vowels that turns Yishai into Ishai, to show how lightly such forms could move. The witnesses thus leave the Gershonite line as a short and partly obscure descent, faithfully copied with its double names and its lost last link, one of the two branches of Levi's eldest whose fuller flowering will appear later in the singer Asaph. Gill's patient equations and Ellicott's cautious guess pull in the same direction: to hold the thread of Gershom's house unbroken even where the names have grown strange, so that when Asaph is set at Heman's right hand the reader may know from what ancient root his song had sprung.
21:
Kohath's line is traced at unusual length in this stretch, and the reformed John Gill explains why: it is drawn out for the sake of Samuel the prophet, who sprang from it. He begins by identifying the Amminadab named here with the Izhar of the law—the posterity of the other Kohathite houses, Amram, Hebron, and Uzziel, being passed over—and then follows the descent through Korah and Assir and Elkanah and Ebiasaph, down through a long chain of Elkanahs and their kin toward the prophet, noting at each turn the alternate names the men bore in other registers: Uriel called Zephaniah, Uzziah the same as Azariah, Shaul whose name is Joel.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch confess the list to be a tangle. The genealogy of Kohath's descendants, they observe, consists of three separate strings of names, each beginning afresh with “the sons of,” yet without showing how the three connect. The very opening is strange, for the law makes Kohath's son Izhar, and Izhar's son Korah (Exod. 6:18-24), whereas here an Amminadab stands at the head, a name found nowhere else as a son of Kohath, though it belongs to a prince among Uzziel's descendants in David's time and to the father-in-law of Aaron in the tribe of Judah (Num. 1:7).

So the witnesses read the Kohathite line as at once the richest and the roughest of the Levite genealogies. Gill smooths it by his patient equations of one name with another, harmonising the variants so that the chain runs unbroken to Samuel; Keil and Delitzsch leave the seams showing, content to mark where the received list departs from the law and where its three fragments have been laid end to end. Both agree on what the labour is for: to prove that Samuel the prophet—and after him the great singer Heman his grandson—was a true Levite of the house of Kohath, so that Israel's foremost prophet and the chief of its temple musicians alike stood in the sacred tribe. The difficulty of the names only underscores the historian's resolve to keep the descent, however worn his sources, rather than let so weighty a pedigree fall.
29:
The house of Merari is given briefest of the three, and the witnesses match the text's economy. The reformed John Gill notes that the posterity of Mushi, Merari's other son, is omitted, and that only the line of Mahli is drawn down—Libni, Shimei, Uzza, Shimea, Haggiah, Asaiah—a short descent of a few generations and no more. Where Kohath's line was spun out for Samuel's sake and Gershom's carried through its double names, Merari's is set down plainly, without cross-reference or comment, the least of the three families in the compass the historian gives it.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch, handling the three Levite lists together, confirm that the descendants of Gershon, Kohath, and Merari are alike in plan, each name linked to the last by “his son,” but that they differ greatly in the number of names preserved. The Merarite string here reaches only to Asaiah, one of the three chiefs of the Levites whom David would set over the service of the sanctuary; and its very shortness, they imply, is a mark of how partial the surviving records were, some houses handing down long pedigrees and others only a handful of links.

The witnesses do not force the list to yield more than it holds. Merari's is the plainest branch of Levi, and they read it as such—a faithful notice of the third great family of the tribe, kept in the register because the whole tribe must be numbered, though this house left no long tale of names behind it. Yet it is not a nameless branch: Mahli and his sons stood in the same sacred calling as their more famous kinsmen, and out of Merari would come Ethan, the third of David's choir-masters, set on the left hand of the singers. So the brief line of Asaiah is not a mere appendix but the root of a share in the temple's song, and the Chronicler keeps it, sparse as it is, that no part of Levi should go uncounted before he turns to the great work of praise that all three houses would divide among them. Keil and Delitzsch's note on the uneven length of the three lists is itself instructive here: it is no defect of the tribe but of the surviving papers, and the historian sets down faithfully what came to his hand, a full pedigree for one house and a bare six names for another, unwilling to invent what his sources had not preserved.
31:
The chapter changes key. Having numbered the families of Levi, the historian turns to the work David assigned them, and the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry opens the theme with a full heart. David was raised up on high to be the sweet psalmist of Israel, not only to pen psalms but to appoint the singing of them in the house of the Lord—not because he was musical so much as because he was devout—and this he did “after that the ark had rest.” While the ark was in captivity, obscure and unsettled, the harps were hung upon the willow-trees and singing was thought unseasonable; but the ark being brought up and settled, the songs were revived and afterward continued, for we should rejoice as much in the prolonging of our spiritual privileges as in the restoring of them.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch fix the phrasing: these are they whom David set “to the hands of the song,” that is, to conduct and carry on the singing, in the house of the Lord from the time the ark, once carried from place to place, received a permanent resting-place on Zion and became the centre of the worship David instituted. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reads the same idiom—literally “made to stand by the sides, or hands, of song,” as if to minister to the sacred music—and compares the recurring phrase in the register of David's musicians (1 Chron. 25:2-3) and the psalm's “as the eyes of servants are unto the hand of their master” (Ps. 123:2). In David's time, he adds, the “house of the Lord” was still a tent, for from its capture by the Philistines the ark had no certain dwelling till David spread for it the tent on Mount Zion (2 Sam. 6:17).

The reformed John Gill only marks where the account of these singers begins, and the wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke brings the Targum's gloss, that David set these men over the service of singing in the house of the sanctuary at the time the ark was brought into it from the house of Obed-edom. The witnesses do not divide; together they make the verse the charter of Israel's choral worship. Henry draws the far horizon that the others leave implicit: when the people of God come to the rest that remains for them above, they shall lay down all their burdens and be employed, like these Levites once the ark had rest, in everlasting songs.
32:
Where the last verse named the singers' commission, this one fixes the place and season of their service. The reformed John Gill explains that they ministered with psalms and hymns and spiritual songs before the ark, which stood in a tent David had pitched for it—the Targum, he notes, calling it a temporary tabernacle, a tabernacle “of time.” The service was rendered before the dwelling until a more lasting house should be built.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott presses the wording closely: “they continued ministering before the dwelling of the Tent of Meeting with the music.” The double name is a defining genitive, like River of Jordan or City of Jerusalem; and the tent of the ark in the city of David is here called by the old name of the Mosaic tabernacle, the “tent of meeting,” of God with man. That ancient tent, he traces, had stood at Shiloh, and at Bethel in the days of the judges (Judg. 20:26-28), at Nob in the reign of Saul, and later at Gibeon (2 Chron. 1:3), while the ark itself now rested apart on Zion. In the court before this sacred dwelling the services of sacrifice and song were carried on together.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch confirm the arrangement and the term. The singing was performed in the court before the dwelling, where the sacrificial worship was, until Solomon had built the house of the Lord in Jerusalem, and then the singers waited on their office according to their order. They add the detail that names the choir's shape: Heman stood in the centre, with Asaph on his right hand and Ethan on his left, so that when the three choirs sang in concert the conducting of the whole fell to Heman. Ellicott would render the closing clause more exactly—“and they stood at their service according to their privilege”—the place and precedence of the choirs having been fixed by David, and standing being the normal posture for the singing; the wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke adds only that this order is the one specified in the verses that follow. So the witnesses leave the verse as a bridge from tent to temple: the same song, begun before the curtains David raised on Zion, was carried unbroken into the more august house of Solomon, its singers keeping their ranks through the change of dwelling.
33:
The long parenthesis closes and the main sentence resumes—“these are they that waited with their children”—and the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott shows what is being resumed. The thread begun at the head of this section, and suspended while the historian described the singers' station, is now taken up: the persons meant are the three chiefs of the Levitical guilds of musicians, Heman, Asaph, and Ethan, and their “children” are the members of those guilds, the word used as in “sons of the prophets” for the men of a prophetic company. The names of the principal sons of the three masters are given in the fuller roster of David's musicians (1 Chron. 25:1-7).

Of the three, Heman is set down first, and Ellicott renders his title not “a singer” but “the singer,” the minstrel—for the original word implies one who sings to his own accompaniment—and calls him first among equals: representing the chief branch of the Levites, his choir held the centre, with the Gershonite Asaph on its right and the Merarite Ethan on its left, so that Heman would conduct the whole body when the three chanted together. The reformed John Gill agrees more briefly, marking these as the three heads, one of each of the three families of Levi, who officiated with their posterity in the service of song.

What most arrests the witnesses is Heman's ancestry, glimpsed already in the words “son of Joel, the son of Shemuel.” Heman the great minstrel, Ellicott notes, was a grandson of Samuel the great prophet—an interesting link between the house of song and the house of prophecy, and, he suggests, an undesigned coincidence that quietly answers those who have doubted whether Samuel was a Levite at all; for here the descent is traced plainly through Kohath to Levi. He recalls the old bond between music and prophecy, the minstrel whose playing brought the hand of the Lord upon Elisha (2 Kings 3:15) and the company of prophets met with a psaltery and a harp (1 Sam. 10:5-6). The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry had made the same connexion tender: Heman, grandson of Samuel, did not think it below him to be a precentor in the house of God, as David himself was willing to be a doorkeeper; and it may be that in setting Samuel's grandson at the head of the choir David had regard to his old friend the prophet. The witnesses thus open the roll of singers on a note of humility and continuity, the prophet's line bending into a life of praise.
39:
At Heman's right hand stood Asaph, and the witnesses trace both his kinship and his office. The reformed John Gill calls him Heman's brother in a double sense: a brother as descended, like him, from Levi, though in the line of Gershom rather than Kohath, and a brother in the same calling, a chief singer set beside him. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott spells out the two bonds—brother as a Levite and brother as a choir-master—and then turns to the pedigree that follows, comparing Asaph's descent with the Gershonite line given earlier. There the list ran only seven names, here thirteen; yet the three names Jahath, Zimmah, and Zerah stand in the same order of descent from Gershom in both, the Adaiah of the one answering to the Iddo of the other, and Ethni not unlike Jeaterai in the Hebrew, so that Asaph's pedigree seems in part already anticipated in the shorter list, genealogies sometimes recurring in abbreviated forms.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch describe Asaph as a descendant of Gershon through the younger son Shimei, and count his genealogy at fifteen names to Gershon, five fewer than Heman's, probably because here and there intermediate links have been dropped. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry recalls that Asaph was more than a singer: several psalms bear his name, whether penned or set by him as chief musician, and he was a seer as well, so that the later record could speak of praising the Lord in the words of David and of Asaph.

Here one witness breaks from the rest. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke, naming Asaph and Heman and their fellows as celebrated for their skill, judges their singing to have been only a kind of recitative or chant such as is still heard in the synagogues, and denies that God had specially appointed either these singers or, the silver trumpets excepted, any musical instruments for his service. He presses the point into open polemic: instruments in the house of God are, at least under the Gospel, repugnant to the spirit of Christianity and tend not a little to corrupt the worship of God, drawing in the light and trifling who love music in the theatre. The other witnesses raise no such quarrel; they leave Asaph honoured at Heman's right hand, singer and seer and psalmist, a true Levite of Gershom's house set in the choir David ordained. Clarke's protest stands apart, a note struck against the very instruments the psalms themselves so often summon to praise.
44:
On the left hand stood the third of the choir-masters, and the reformed John Gill introduces him with his fellows: their brethren the sons of Merari, the brethren of the Kohathites and Gershonites as descending from the same ancestor Levi. Ethan is his name, of the youngest of the three Levite houses, set to balance Heman in the centre and Asaph on the right, that the whole tribe might have its part in the song.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley adds the identification that ties this Ethan to a familiar name: he is called also Jeduthun, as the later registers show (1 Chron. 9:16) and as the titles of several psalms attest, the two names belonging to one man. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch place him in the younger Merarite line of Mushi and note a variation in his father's name—here Kishi, but Kushaiah in the parallel (1 Chron. 15:17)—the latter, they judge, the fuller and original form, one more of the slight shifts of spelling that run through these lists.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry had already set Ethan beside Heman as one whose pedigree is here traced all the way up to Levi, and raised the question that hangs over both men: whether this Ethan and this Heman are the same as the Ezrahites named in the titles of two psalms (Ps. 88 and Ps. 89). Henry finds no evident reason why these Levites should be called Ezrahites, as there is for those elsewhere reckoned among the sons of Zerah, and leaves the puzzle open. The witnesses converge on the main thing: with Ethan on the left, the choir is complete, one master from each family of Levi—Heman the Kohathite, Asaph the Gershonite, Ethan the Merarite—so that the burden and the honour of the sacred song fell evenly on the whole tribe, and every house knew its post. The very evenness, Gill and Henry feel, was of David's design, that no branch of Levi should be without its share in the praise of the house of God. Wesley's small note that Ethan is Jeduthun is not idle, for it joins this bare genealogical name to a living psalmody: under both names the man is remembered as a chief musician whose sons prophesied with harps and cymbals in the service David appointed, so that the left-hand choir, like the other two, has left its voice in the Psalter itself.
48:
Not every Levite could sing, and this verse provides for the rest. The reformed John Gill reads it of the Levites who were not skilled in the service of song—the remaining Kohathites, Gershonites, and Merarites—who were appointed to the other labours of the house of God. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott presses the key word: “appointed” is literally “given,” the term nethunim, the Levites given to Aaron and his sons as their assistants, as the law says, “given are they to him from among the sons of Israel” (Num. 3:9)—the same form, he notes, that names the class of temple-servants called Nethinim. The dwelling here, he adds, is the mishkan, the sanctuary they were set to serve.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch confirm that these other Levites, besides the singers just named, were given for every service of the dwelling of the house of God, made over to the priests for the carrying on of the worship (Num. 8:16-19). The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry fills in what such service was: the abundance of work about the tabernacle—to provide water and fuel, to wash and sweep and carry out ashes, to kill and flay and boil the sacrifices—and to all this the Levites of the other families were appointed, or perhaps those not fit to be singers, that had no good voice or ear.

Henry draws the lesson the verse quietly teaches. As every one has received the gift, so let him minister: those who could not sing were not therefore laid aside as good for nothing, for though unfit for that service there was other service they might be useful in. The witnesses do not divide; they read the verse as the charter of the humbler Levite work, and find in it a pattern for the church—that a body has many members and many offices, and the man who cannot lead the song may yet carry the wood and draw the water, and be as truly given to God's house as the precentor at its head. Ellicott's philology and Henry's homily meet in the one point: these were given men, their whole life a gift laid on the altar of the sanctuary's daily need.
49:
Above the singers and the servants stood the priests, and this verse marks off their peculiar work. The reformed John Gill states the division simply: Aaron and his sons, those who descended from him, though of the same tribe of Levi, were all priests, set apart from their Levite brethren for the altar. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders the opening as a participle of unbroken action—“Aaron and his sons were offering”—and takes “Aaron and his sons” for the technical name of the priesthood, to whom three functions belonged: sacrifice upon the altars of burnt-offering and of incense, the work of the most holy place, and atonement for Israel by the appointed rites.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch set out the same three offices and note that all was done “according to all that Moses the servant of God had commanded,” a phrase governing the whole ministry of the priests. Ellicott lingers over that title. After his death Moses is thrice called the servant of the Lord (Deut. 34:5), faithful as a servant in God's house (Heb. 3:5), foreshadowing in grand if imperfect outline that other servant of the Lord of whom the later prophets speak; and the Chronicler writes “servant of God” with the divine name held, in his day, in ever-increasing awe.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry draws the theology of the office out of its two ends. The priests were to make atonement for Israel, to mediate between the people and God—not to magnify or enrich themselves but to serve the public, ordained for men. And they were to do it by rule, presiding in God's house yet doing as they were bidden, according to all that God commanded, a law to which the very highest are subject. Henry marks how the offices needed one another: the singers, the serving Levites, and the sacrificing priests each had their work, and both needed and helped one another in it. The witnesses close the account of the sanctuary's ministers on this height—the priest at the altar making atonement—and the reader who has followed the singers and the servants sees the whole tribe of Levi ranged in one ordered service, every man in his place, all of it pointing beyond itself to the one great High Priest through whom alone atonement is truly made.
54:
The register moves from persons to places, from the men of Levi to the towns given them to dwell in, and the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry seizes on the word the text uses—these cities are called their castles. Not only because they were walled and fortified and guarded by the country, for it is every nation's interest to protect its ministers, but because the Levites and their possessions were in a special manner the care of divine providence: as God was their portion, so God was their protection, and a cottage will be a castle to those who abide under the shadow of the Almighty.

The reformed John Gill treats the list as an account of the dwelling-places assigned the priests and Levites in the several tribes, agreeing with the older record in Joshua with some few changes of name, and he flags a defect at the outset: two cities, Juttah and Gibeon, have dropped out through careless copying, since the sum is given as thirteen while only eleven are named. He notes too that the suburbs allotted each city ran two thousand cubits about it, both for the cities of refuge and the rest. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read the opening as a superscription over the whole catalogue—“these are their dwelling-places according to their districts, in their boundaries”—the word for “district” meaning properly a ring of encampments, and the clause that follows, about the sons of Aaron of the family of Kohath and the lot that fell to them, a near repetition of Joshua's account (Josh. 21:10), where the word “first” stands that has here perhaps been lost.

On that lot the wesleyan-arminian witnesses agree with the grammarians. Adam Clarke explains that all the tribes obtained their inheritance by lot, but that to the sons of Aaron fell the first lot, as the Syriac and Arabic understand it, Judah drawing that first lot so that the priestly cities might be given out of its ground; and John Wesley, echoing Henry on the castles, adds the same gloss, “the lot,” or rather the first lot. Henry gathers the providence in the whole arrangement: God took care by it to fulfil dying Jacob's word that Levi should be scattered in Israel (Gen. 49:7), to diffuse the knowledge of his law to every corner of the land—every tribe furnished with its Levites, as every room with a candle—and to provide a comfortable maintenance for those who ministered in holy things. The witnesses thus open the city-list not as dry geography but as the map of a scattered ministry, the tribe without a land of its own planted in forty-eight towns that the light might reach all Israel.
5:
The high-priestly line runs on in a chain of bare begettings—Abishua, Bukki, Uzzi, Zerahiah, and the rest—and the grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch carry the whole descent, in their comment on this stretch, from Abishua down through two-and-twenty names to its end. The sequence is not idle repetition but a record of succession: the son named after his father, living on when the father died, inherited the high-priestly dignity, so that the list is in effect the roll of Israel's chief priests from the wilderness to the fall of Jerusalem.

Keil and Delitzsch fix the two ends of the chain. It begins with Eleazar, who came out of Egypt, and it closes with Jehozadak, of whom the text will say that he went into captivity when the Lord carried Judah and Jerusalem away by the hand of Nebuchadnezzar. That Jehozadak, they note, was the father of the Joshua who returned from exile with Zerubbabel and served as first high priest in the restored community (Ezra 3:2; Hag. 1:1), so that the line broken off by the captivity is picked up again beyond it. The word “went forth,” they observe, must be filled out from the following clause as “went into exile.”

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry had already drawn the meaning of these two boundaries into a single stroke. The line begins with Eleazar, who came out of the house of bondage in Egypt, and ends with Jehozadak, who went into the house of bondage in Babylon; thus, for their sins, the people were left as they were found, delivered out of one servitude only to be carried into another. It might also intimate, Henry adds, that the Levitical priesthood did not make anything perfect, but that this was to be done by the bringing in of a better hope. The witnesses do not divide; the grammarian who counts the generations and the preacher who reads their shape agree that this dry list of names is a quiet sermon—a priesthood that ran unbroken for centuries yet could not keep its people from bondage, and so pointed past itself to a priest and a redemption it could not supply.
17:
The grandsons of Levi are set out, house by house—of Gershom, Libni and Shimei; of Kohath, Amram, Izhar, Hebron, and Uzziel; of Merari, Mahli and Mushi—and the grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch use the verse to describe the make of the whole section. It is introduced, they note, by this enumeration of the sons and grandsons of Levi, and then unfolds in the three parallel lists of descent; and the three lists are alike in plan, each name knit to the one before it by the little word “his son,” though they run to very different lengths. The verse before us is the seed-bed of all that follows, naming the heads from whom the families of Gershom, Kohath, and Merari take their rise.

The reformed John Gill had marked at the head of this section that the sons of Levi were repeated precisely for the sake of these grandsons, whose names the next verses supply; and the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry recalls how conversant the reader of the earlier books is with just these names—Gershom, Kohath, and Merari—which stand so large in the book of Numbers, where the families of the Levites were marshalled and had their several charges of the tabernacle assigned them.

The witnesses read the verse, then, as the orderly foundation of the Levite register. Nothing here is new; the names were all rehearsed in the wilderness, when this tribe was numbered apart and given its work about the sanctuary. But the historian sets them down again because the whole tribe must be shown springing from one father through these three sons and their households, so that when the long descents diverge—one running to Samuel and Heman, another to Asaph, a third to Ethan—the reader may hold in view the common root from which they all branch. Keil and Delitzsch's stress on the uniform pattern of the lists, each generation linked to the last, makes the point plain: the Chronicler is drawing a single tree, and this verse names the three great limbs before he follows each to its farthest twig. It is a register built for clarity, that amid so many strange names the shape of Levi's house should never be lost.
23:
Kohath's line resumes with a fresh string of names—Elkanah, Ebiasaph, Assir, Tahath, Uriel, Uzziah, Shaul—and the grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch treat it as one of the three fragments that make up the Kohathite descent, each beginning anew and none showing plainly how it joins the others. When the list turns next to “the sons of Elkanah, Amasai and Ahimoth,” they take it that a new branch begins, and reason that this Elkanah is likely the brother of the Abiasaph named just before; yet a comparison with Heman's pedigree, where Elkanah, Amasai, and Mahath stand in order, suggests rather that Ahimoth is only another form of Mahath, and the two series in truth one.

The reformed John Gill, tracing this same stretch as part of the long line he draws down to Samuel, quietly resolves the tangles by his equations—Uriel he takes for the Zephaniah of the parallel, Uzziah for Azariah, Shaul for Joel—so that the chain, however its names shift from list to list, runs on unbroken toward the prophet who is its goal. Where Keil and Delitzsch leave the joints exposed and weigh how the fragments fit, Gill smooths them into a single descent.

The witnesses agree that the difficulty is real and the purpose plain. These are the middle links of the Kohathite line, the generations between Korah and the house of Elkanah out of which Samuel and Heman would come; and though the copies differ in the forms of the names and the points where the sub-lists are welded together, both witnesses hold the substance firm. Keil and Delitzsch's care to match name against name across the two registers, and Gill's patience in harmonising them, serve the one end: to show that the great singer set at the head of the temple choir stood in a true and traceable descent from Kohath the son of Levi, even where the sources the Chronicler used had grown worn and hard to align. The seams in the list, far from discrediting it, are the marks of an honest compiler keeping what he received. And the labour is not spent on obscure men only: it is the pedigree of Samuel that is being secured through these very links, so that the prophet who anointed Israel's kings, and the singer who led the temple choir after him, are anchored past dispute in the house of Kohath the son of Levi.
26:
A knot in the Kohathite list draws the fullest attention here, and the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sets himself to untie it. The Hebrew as it stands reads oddly—“Elkanah his son, Elkanah, Zophai his son”—and Ellicott, following the Greek and the Syriac, judges the second Elkanah an intrusion, crept in perhaps from a scribe who remembered the Elkanah of Ramah, where Zophim stands just before the name (1 Sam. 1:1). Strike it out, and the line runs clear, matching Heman's pedigree, where Elkanah is son of Mahath, son of Amasai.

Ellicott presses the comparison through the whole run of names. Zophai stands to Zuph as Chelubai to Chelub, a fuller by-form of the same name; Nahath looks like a transformation of the Toah of the ascending list; Eliab—“El is father”—is a by-form of Eliel, “El is God”; and if Mahath be the same as the Ahimoth of the verse before, then the true reading would run “Amasai his son, Ahimoth his son, Elkanah his son, Zophai his son,” and the two series, descending and ascending, coincide at every step. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch had reached the same suspicion from the other side, that this Elkanah is the son of the Joel named in Heman's line, and Ahimoth without difficulty another form of Mahath.

The witnesses do not quarrel; both grammarians are working the same seam, and both conclude that the doubled Elkanah and the shifting spellings are copyists' work, not two different families. What looks at first like confusion is, on their reading, a single genealogy preserved in two shapes, once counted downward from Kohath and once upward from Heman, the very disagreements of form becoming, when patiently compared, the proof that it is one line and not two. Ellicott's eye for the by-forms of a name—Zophai and Zuph, Nahath and Toah, Eliab and Eliel—turns the roughness of the text into evidence of its fidelity: the Chronicler kept what his documents gave him, and the labour of harmonising them only shows how genuinely ancient and how independently transmitted those documents were. The knot the grammarians patiently loose is thus no flaw in the record but a fingerprint of its age, the two forms of one house surviving side by side because the historian would rather copy faithfully than tidy away the witness of his sources.
34:
Heman's descent is now traced the other way, climbing from the singer himself back through Elkanah and Jeroham and Eliel and Toah toward the tribe's father, and the grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch follow the ascent link by link—through Zuph and Elkanah and Mahath and Amasai, through another Elkanah and Joel and Azariah and Zephaniah, and on by Tahath, Assir, Ebiasaph, Korah, and Izhar to Kohath, to Levi, to Israel himself. It is the same house already counted downward, now set out in the ascending form that anchors the great musician in the trunk of the nation.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott had prepared this reading, noting that Heman's pedigree, agreeing so closely with the Kohathite list given earlier, shows the singer's Levitical descent beyond doubt, and that the many small variants between the two forms are the natural marks of lists drawn from different documents. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry supplies the human warmth the bare ascent leaves unspoken: this Heman, whose line here reaches back to Israel, was grandson of Samuel the prophet and son of that Joel of whom it had been said he walked not in his father's ways (1 Sam. 8:2-3); yet the blessing entailed on the seed of the upright, passing over one generation, fastened on the next, and the prophet's grandson became a precentor in the house of God.

The witnesses converge on the weight of that closing word, “the son of Levi, the son of Israel.” The list does not stop at Kohath but runs up to the patriarch, and in so doing it binds the temple's chief musician to the whole covenant people: Heman leads the song not as a private virtuoso but as a true son of Israel, standing in a descent as old as the nation. Keil and Delitzsch's careful tally of the generations and Ellicott's demonstration that this ascending line and the earlier descending one are one and the same serve Henry's tender point—that God carries his purposes down through the generations, sometimes leaping a faithless link to rest on a faithful heir, until the prophet's house yields a leader of praise whose voice is planted in the very root of Israel.
40:
Asaph's ancestry climbs by its own stair—through Michael and Baaseiah and Malchiah, through Ethni and Zerah and Adaiah, through Ethan and Zimmah and Shimei and Jahath—up to Gershom and to Levi, and the grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch carry the ascent to its head, marking that where Heman's line reached Israel this one halts at Levi, since it was enough to show the Gershonite singer sprung from the sacred tribe. The line is shorter than Heman's by several names, some intermediate links having dropped away.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott had already drawn the threads together at the head of Asaph's pedigree, and his observations govern the reading of this ascent. Three of its names—Jahath, Zimmah, and Zerah—stand in the same order of descent from Gershom as in the shorter Gershonite list given earlier; the Adaiah here answers to the Iddo there, and the Ethni is in Hebrew not unlike the Jeaterai; so that Asaph's line seems in part to have been anticipated already, genealogies now and then recurring in abbreviated forms. The agreement, he judges, is too close to be accidental.

The witnesses thus read the verse as one more instance of the register's method and its fidelity. The same house appears twice, once in a brief seven-name notice among the Gershonite families and once in this fuller ascending pedigree of the singer, and the partial overlap of names is not a blunder but the sign of a common source unevenly preserved. Keil and Delitzsch's patient climb to Levi and Ellicott's matching of the recurring names make the same point that has governed the whole chapter: that beneath the tangle of variant spellings and unequal lists there runs one true descent, and that Asaph, like Heman, is shown by it to be no mere musician drafted into the choir but a genuine Levite of Gershom's house, his song rooted in the tribe God set apart for the service of his sanctuary. That a house should be counted once briefly among the families and again at length behind its singer is, on their showing, exactly what an honest register drawn from many papers would look like—not a seamless invention, but a faithful gathering of what the fathers had kept, the small overlaps binding the two forms into one.
45:
Ethan's line ascends last of the three, and the grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch trace it up from Hashabiah through Amaziah and Hilkiah, through Amzi and Bani and Shamer, to Mahli, Mushi, Merari, and Levi—the shortest of the three singers' pedigrees, yet reaching, like the others, to the father of the tribe. With this the roll of the choir-masters is complete, each of the three shown by his own ascending line to spring from Levi.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry had noted that this Ethan, like Heman before him, has his pedigree traced up here as high as Levi, so that the reader may see the Merarite singer as truly of the sacred tribe as his fellows at the centre and the right. And the wesleyan-arminian John Wesley's identification of Ethan with Jeduthun hangs over the ascent, giving the bare climb of names a face and a psalmody, for under that name the man is remembered among the chief musicians whose sons served with harp and cymbal in the house of the Lord.

The witnesses read the verse as the closing of a deliberate pattern. Three times now the historian has set a singer at the foot of a genealogy and led it upward to Levi—Heman the Kohathite to Israel himself, Asaph the Gershonite to Levi, and Ethan the Merarite here to Levi again—so that the three houses of the tribe are each represented in the sanctuary's song and each proved, name by name, to stand in the covenant descent. Keil and Delitzsch's sober tracing of the shortest line to its root completes the symmetry: no branch of Levi is without its voice in the choir, and none is without its warrant in the pedigree. The very brevity of Ethan's list, set beside the length of Heman's, is one more token of sources unevenly kept, but it takes nothing from the point the whole arrangement was built to make—that the praise of God's house rose from the whole tribe, ordered and rooted, with every master singer a son of Levi. The three ascending lines, ending together at the common father, are the historian's way of saying that the song was no accident of talent but a charge laid on Levi itself, discharged by each of its houses in turn.
51:
The second register of high priests continues its short ascent—Bukki, Uzzi, Zerahiah, Meraioth, Amariah, Ahitub, Zadok, Ahimaaz—and the grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch, who had opened this list a verse before, read it as a second and briefer roll of the sons of Aaron, running from Eleazar down to Ahimaaz the son of Zadok. It is cast, they note, in the same form as the family-lists of the singers, each name joined to the last by “his son,” and drawn most likely from the same source, set here to make the transition from the priests to the register of their cities that follows.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke had marked, at the head of this list, that it is a second account of Aaron's sons, less extensive than the fuller genealogy given earlier in the chapter, and he draws from the doubling a conclusion about the historian's method: the writer plainly had several tables before him, from which he borrowed as he judged proper, so that the same descent could be given twice, once at length and once in brief. The repetition is no oversight but the natural result of a compiler working from more than one document.

The witnesses agree on the modest purpose the list serves. It carries the high-priestly line only as far as Ahimaaz, the son of that Zadok whom David advanced, and stops—for Ahimaaz, Keil and Delitzsch observe elsewhere, probably succeeded his father in Solomon's day, and it is his father's house whose dwelling-places the next verses will number. So this shorter roll is not a rival to the great list that reached to the captivity but a stepping-stone: it names the priests of the early monarchy just far enough to reach the men to whom the first of the Levitical cities were given, and then hands the narrative over to the geography of the tribe's inheritance. Clarke's remark on the several tables and Keil and Delitzsch's on the shared form and function meet in the one judgment—that the Chronicler, faithful to his varied sources, set down what each supplied, even at the cost of telling the priestly descent a second time.
55:
The first and greatest of the Levitical cities is Hebron, and the witnesses note both its gift and the older record from which the notice is copied. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch observe that this verse and the next answer almost word for word to the account in Joshua (Josh. 21:11-12), and that the whole catalogue which follows runs parallel to that chapter, so that their task is chiefly to gather the errors and defects that have crept into the present text. Here they let the verse stand: Hebron in the land of Judah, with its suburbs round about, given to the sons of Aaron.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott fills in the differences of wording between the two records. Where Chronicles says simply “Hebron,” Joshua names it “the city of Arba the father of Anak, that is, Hebron”; where this text has “the land of Judah,” Joshua reads “the hill-country,” a single letter parting the two Hebrew words. The “suburbs,” he explains, are properly the pastures or open commons about the town, as opposed to the arable fields, and the law had fixed their extent—so many cubits measured out on every side of the city (Num. 35:3-5)—so that each Levitical city carried its ring of grazing land with it.

The witnesses read the verse as the fitting head of the whole list. Hebron was no mean town—the ancient city of the giants, the burial-place of the patriarchs, the first seat of David's throne—and it is given first, and to the priests, the sons of Aaron. Keil and Delitzsch's care to align the notice with Joshua and Ellicott's attention to the pastures and the variant names serve the same end: to show that this is no new grant but the faithful recording of an ancient allotment, the priestly tribe endowed not with a territory of its own but with chosen cities and their commons scattered through the land. The gift of Hebron sets the pattern for all that follows, a ministry housed among the people it served, and provided, down to the measured pasture at its gate, for the maintenance of those who had no other inheritance but the Lord.
56:
A careful exception is noted before the list goes on: though Hebron itself was given the priests, the fields of the city and its villages were not. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch record that these open lands were given to Caleb the son of Jephunneh, and pass at once to the cities of Judah assigned the sons of Aaron—Hebron the city of refuge, Libnah, Jattir, Eshtemoa, and the rest—pausing to mark the flaws that have entered the text. The plural “cities of refuge” is wrong, they say, for of all those named only Hebron was such a city, and Joshua rightly has the singular; and after Jattir the usual clause “with her suburbs” has fallen away.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott adds the small supplement that Joshua carries and Chronicles drops: there the grant of the fields to Caleb is called his “possession,” the word of settled ownership. The distinction the verse preserves is a nice one—the town and its pasture went to the Levites for their dwelling, but the farmland round about remained Caleb's by the older promise—and the witnesses value it as a sign of how exactly the allotment was framed, giving the priests a place to live and their flocks to graze without dispossessing the great Judahite of the inheritance won for him at the conquest.

So the witnesses read the verse as a study in the precision of the arrangement and in the wear of the manuscript alike. Caleb keeps his fields; the priests keep their city and its commons; and the text, faithfully copied, carries even its own blemishes—the mistaken plural, the dropped clause—which Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott quietly set right against the parallel in Joshua. The care they take is itself instructive: the historian did not invent a tidy scheme but transmitted an ancient record, and where his copy had slipped they restore it not by conjecture but by the witness of the older book. The gift to Caleb, set as an aside in the midst of the priestly cities, keeps faith with the promise made to him long before, and shows that the endowing of Levi took nothing by force from the other tribes but was fitted, town by town and field by field, into an order already just.
58:
The list of priestly cities is completed—Hilen and Debir, Ashan and Bethshemesh out of Judah, and out of Benjamin, Geba, Alemeth, and Anathoth—and the grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch reckon the total and mend the gaps. The cities of the sons of Aaron are summed as thirteen, yet only eleven are here named; before Bethshemesh the name Juttah has been lost, and before Geba the name Gibeon, so that the count falls two short of its own sum. They gather too the variant spellings: Hilen stands for the Holon of Joshua, Ashan for the more correct Ain, Alemeth for Almon, the same towns under shifted forms.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott agrees on the names and adds their meaning. Hilen, he says, is the Holon that twice occurs in Joshua, the more natural form; and Debir—the town anciently called Kirjath-sepher, the “Book Town”—bears a name that means “oracle,” the inmost sanctuary, as if the very map of Levi's cities were lettered with the words of worship. The two grammarians read the same short list the same way, noting where the copy has slipped and restoring the missing towns from the parallel record.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry had prepared the reader for just such differences of spelling, warning that it does no hurt to the credit of Scripture if the names of some places be not spelt here as they were in Joshua, since it is common for cities to have several names and time changes names, as our own towns are known by more than one. The witnesses thus refuse to be troubled by the roughness of the register. Thirteen cities were given the priests out of Judah and Simeon and Benjamin, the sacred tribe planted thick about the very heart of the land and near the future temple; and though two names have dropped from the copy and others have changed their shape, the sum stands and the parallel supplies the rest. Keil and Delitzsch's arithmetic and Ellicott's etymology and Henry's calm meet in one judgment—that the list is a true and ancient record, its little wounds no bar to its plain testimony that the priests were given cities and pastures in the closest tribes, that the altar's servants might dwell among the people they served.
62:
Turning from the priests to the rest of Levi, this verse portions out the Gershonites and Merarites among the tribes, and the grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch follow the reckoning. The sons of Gershom received thirteen cities out of Issachar, Asher, Naphtali, and Manasseh in Bashan; the sons of Merari, twelve, by lot, out of Reuben, Gad, and Zebulun; and then the children of Israel gave the Levites all these cities with their suburbs. They note, however, that the two verses which sum the gift to the whole tribe are here out of their proper place—their true home is higher up, among the opening words of the catalogue—and that they stand as a superscription in Joshua (Josh. 21:8-9) before the naming of the priestly towns, though how the confusion arose in the present text cannot be certainly told.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott attends to the wording. “Gershom,” which Joshua writes as Gershon, is followed by a phrase best read “with regard to their clans”; and where this text says “the tribe of Manasseh in Bashan,” Joshua more exactly has the “half-tribe.” The differences are small, but Ellicott marks them as further tokens that the Chronicler drew on a source of his own, close to Joshua yet not simply copied from it.

The witnesses read the verse as the frame that gathers the scattered grants into one. Here the totals are struck—thirteen cities to Gershom, twelve to Merari, over against the thirteen already given the priests—and the whole is set under the note that Israel gave these towns to the Levites, so that the tribe's inheritance is seen to be the free gift of the other tribes out of their own possessions. Keil and Delitzsch's patience with the displaced verses and Ellicott's eye for the variant forms serve the same reading: the register is an ancient and honestly transmitted document, its order sometimes disturbed and its spellings sometimes shifted, but its substance firm—the landless tribe of Levi settled by lot and by gift throughout the twelve, that no corner of Israel should be without the ministers of God near at hand.
65:
The lot is cast again, and out of Judah, Simeon, and Benjamin the named cities are given, while the residue of the Kohathites receive their towns out of Ephraim. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read the verse as introducing the cities of the remaining Kohathites, answering to the like passage in Joshua (Josh. 21:20-26), and correct a small slip of the preposition, which as it stands gives no fit sense: the meaning is that, as regards the families of the sons of Kohath, the cities of their holding lay in the tribe of Ephraim, and these were given them. They note again the mistaken plural “cities of refuge” and observe that before one of the following verses a whole line has dropped out of the text.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke fastens on the closing phrase, that these cities were “called by their names.” Probably, he suggests, each family gave its own name to the city that fell to its lot, so that the very map of Levi's holdings would carry the memory of the households settled in them—a homely touch that makes the register something more than a table of the lot's cold arithmetic.

The witnesses read the verse as the turning of the catalogue from the priests to their Kohathite kinsmen. The same solemn method governs it—the lot cast, the cities named, the tribes drawn upon—and the same marks of a worn but honest text appear: the misplaced word, the lost line, the plural set right against Joshua. Keil and Delitzsch labour to recover the sense where the copy has failed, and Clarke turns the bare notice human with his guess about the naming of the towns. Together they keep the reader mindful that this is no dry invention but the record of a real settlement, the households of Kohath planted by God's own lot among Judah and Simeon and Benjamin and Ephraim, their names written into the land they were given, that the ministry of the sanctuary might be spread and rooted through the whole inheritance of Israel. Even the lost line and the mistaken plural, faithfully carried and quietly mended, testify that the Chronicler handed on his source rather than polish it, so that the reader receives the allotment as the ancients kept it.
67:
The remaining Kohathites receive their cities, and among them the historian marks the places of refuge. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch enumerate them in the domain of Ephraim and the half-tribe of Manasseh—Shechem in mount Ephraim, Gezer, Jokmeam, Bethhoron, Aijalon, Gathrimmon, and, out of Manasseh, Aner and Bileam—and then pass to the Gershonites, who received out of the half-tribe Golan in Bashan and Ashtaroth, the list answering throughout to Joshua (Josh. 21:27-33). They flag the corruptions as they go: further on, a Kedesh stands where the parallel has Kishion, and a Ramoth where it more correctly reads Jarmuth.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott corrects the opening clause, which the received text has blurred. It should read, he says, “they gave unto them the cities of refuge, Shechem and her pastures, in the hill-country of Ephraim, and Gezer and her pastures”—and here again the plural misleads, for Gezer was no city of refuge, and both here and above the singular “city” is likely the original word. He counts these among the many marks of textual corruption that run through this section, the same inaccuracy that recurred in the notice of the priestly cities.

The witnesses read the verse as one more panel of the great allotment, given over to the Kohathites who were not of Aaron's line and to the Gershonites beside them. What most engages the grammarians is the recurring flaw—the loose plural of “cities of refuge” fastened onto towns that were none—and their patient restoring of it against Joshua. Yet the substance holds: Shechem, a true asylum for the manslayer, heads the Ephraimite cities, and the sacred tribe is planted in the central hills and across the Jordan alike. Keil and Delitzsch's tracing of the parallel and Ellicott's correction of the text meet in the conviction that has carried through the whole register—that a copy may slip in a word and still hand down a true account, and that the labour of setting it right is itself a service to the record, not a reproach against it. And the towns themselves are not without dignity: Shechem in the hill-country, Bethhoron and Aijalon in the pass, Golan and Ashtaroth in Bashan were places of weight and memory, given now to the sacred tribe that its ministry might stand where the nation's life and history ran thickest.
72:
Northward the roll of the Gershonite and Merarite cities runs on—out of Issachar, Kedesh, Daberath, Ramoth, and Anem; out of Asher, Mashal, Abdon, Hukok, and Rehob; out of Naphtali, Kedesh in Galilee, Hammon, and Kirjathaim—and the grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch follow it into the tribe of Zebulun, where the rest of the Merarites received their portion. Here they mark a defect: only two cities, Rimmon and Tabor, are named in Zebulun, where the parallel in Joshua (Josh. 21:34-37) has four, two having dropped from the present text. The phrase “to the sons of Merari the remaining” they read as marking these off as the last of the Levites still to be numbered.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke, surveying this whole stretch of names, grants how little it seems to yield: how barren, he says, is such a register to us, both of incident and of interest. Yet he answers his own complaint. As barren rocks and sandy deserts make integral and necessary parts of the globe, so do these genealogical tables make necessary parts of the history of God's providence and grace in the maintenance of truth and the building of the Church; and therefore no one who fears God will despise or lightly esteem them.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry's reading of the whole allotment presides over these northern towns as over the southern. God took care by such a scattering, he had said, to fulfil dying Jacob's word that Levi should be divided in Israel (Gen. 49:7), and to diffuse the knowledge of his law to every part of the land, so that Galilee and the vale of Jezreel, as much as Judah, had their Levite cities and their teaching light. The witnesses thus keep the dry list from being dead. Keil and Delitzsch mend its arithmetic, Clarke defends its worth against the reader's impatience, and Henry unfolds its design; and between them the towns of Issachar and Asher and Naphtali and Zebulun become not a lifeless catalogue but the visible reach of a ministry meant to touch the farthest tribes, the light set in every room of the house of Israel.
78:
Across the Jordan the register moves at last, to the eastern tribes, and the grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch trace the Merarite cities there. Beyond Jordan by Jericho, on the east side of the river, out of Reuben were given Bezer in the wilderness and Jahzah, with Kedemoth and Mephaath to follow. They note that these two verses are wanting in some editions of Joshua but are found in many manuscripts and the oldest printed copies, dropped only by an oversight; and they point the reader to the fuller notices of these towns elsewhere—Bezer, the city of refuge in the wilderness (Deut. 4:43), and Jahzah and its fellows in the law's account of Reuben's land (Josh. 13:18).

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke's word over the whole of this dry ground applies here with special force. Barren as such a register is of incident and interest, he had said, these tables are yet as needful to the history of providence as barren rocks and sandy deserts are to the globe; and none who fears God will lightly esteem them. The eastern cities, remote and half-lost even from the copies of Joshua, are a case in point—easily overlooked, yet kept, because no part of the Levite inheritance was to be let fall.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry's sense of the design holds good on this far side of the river as on the near. The scattering of Levi through every tribe, east and west alike, was God's provision that the knowledge of his law should reach all Israel, and that the tribes beyond Jordan—Reuben and Gad, so often felt to be on the edge of the nation—should have the ministers of God settled among them too. The witnesses converge on the quiet lesson of the verse: even here, in the wilderness pastures of Reuben, in towns the manuscripts nearly lost, the sacred tribe was planted, that Bezer the refuge and its neighbour cities might carry the worship and the law of God to the outermost bounds of the inheritance. That the copies of Joshua nearly let these verses slip, and yet the Chronicler's record preserved them, is itself a small parable of the care with which the whole allotment was handed down.
79:
The trans-Jordan cities continue—Kedemoth and Mephaath out of Reuben, and out of Gad, Ramoth in Gilead and Mahanaim—and the grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch carry the notice through, pointing as before to the older accounts of these towns: Ramoth in Gilead, itself a city of refuge (Josh. 21:36), and Mahanaim, the place of Jacob's angels and later of David's exile, with Heshbon and Jazer to close the list (Josh. 13:26). The names are given plainly, the register nearing its end.

The witnesses find these eastern towns weightier than their bare mention suggests. Ramoth in Gilead was no obscure village but a fortress long contested with Syria and a city of refuge for the manslayer; Mahanaim a royal town of refuge in its own turn; so that the Merarites were set, here as elsewhere, in places of consequence, that the ministry might dwell where the life of the nation was thickest. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke's defence of the whole catalogue bears repeating over just such a verse: barren of interest as the list of names may seem, it is a necessary part of the history of God's grace, and no one who fears him will hold it cheap.

So the witnesses read the verse as part of the sober, deliberate closing of the register. The lot had reached across the Jordan, and Gad no less than Judah had its Levite cities, its towns of refuge, its dwellings for the servants of the altar. Keil and Delitzsch's steady cross-reference to the law and to Joshua keeps the reader aware that nothing here is new invention: these are the same cities the older books had named, gathered once more that the record of Levi's inheritance might be complete. The scattering of the tribe, begun in Judah and traced through the northern lands, is here carried to its eastern bound, so that from Hebron in the south to Ramoth in Gilead beyond the river the whole land is seen ringed with the cities of God's ministers, and the landless tribe shown, city by city, richly provided by the tribes among whom it dwelt.
81:
The register closes, as it began, on a bare pair of names—Heshbon with her suburbs, and Jazer with her suburbs—and the grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch simply set them down, the last of the cities of Gad given the Merarites, with the pastures that belonged to every Levitical town. The two towns of Reuben and Gad beyond Jordan bring the long catalogue of forty-eight cities to its end, and with it the whole genealogy of Levi that has run from the sons of the patriarch to the towns of his scattered descendants.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke's reflection on the whole is the fittest word over its close. How barren, he had granted, such a register seems to us, of incident and of interest; and yet, as barren rocks and sandy deserts are integral and necessary parts of the globe, so these genealogical tables are necessary parts of the history of God's providence and grace in the maintenance of truth and the establishing of the Church, and therefore no one who fears God will either despise or lightly esteem them. The chapter that could seem the driest in Scripture ends, on Clarke's reading, as a quiet testimony to the faithfulness of God.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry gathers the design that the last verse silently completes. In appointing these cities God took care to fulfil dying Jacob's prophecy that Levi should be scattered in Israel (Gen. 49:7), to diffuse the knowledge of his law to every part of the land, and to provide a comfortable maintenance for those who ministered in holy things; every tribe had benefit of the Levites, and so every tribe contributed to their support. The witnesses leave the chapter here, on Heshbon and Jazer, with the tribe of Levi wholly numbered and wholly settled—priests and singers and servants traced to their father, and their dwellings traced through all Israel—a landless people whose portion was the Lord, planted in forty-eight cities that the light of his law might reach, from the hill-country of Judah to the pastures beyond Jordan, into every corner of the inheritance. So the longest and driest chapter of the book ends, on the witnesses' reading, as a monument of God's faithfulness—the tribe of the altar counted and housed to the last town, and the covenant kept in the very geography of the land.
4:
Eleazar's succession opens the high-priestly roll, and the wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke explains at once why the historian holds to this one branch. Eleazar begat Phinehas, and because the high priesthood continued in this family for so long a time, the sacred writer confines himself chiefly to it, omitting Nadab and Abihu, and even the whole house of Ithamar. The line is narrowed on purpose: it is the descent of the chief priests, not a full account of Aaron's posterity, that the register means to give.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch had drawn out the same purpose in their view of the whole stretch. From the older commentators they take the right inference: that the enumeration here is meant to communicate the succession of high priests, the son so begotten, when he outlived his father, inheriting the office. Phinehas the son of Eleazar, they note, is already found in possession of it in the days of the judges (Judg. 20:28), the same Phinehas whose zeal at Peor the law had recorded (Num. 25:11).

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry gathers the theological weight of the line whose first links are here laid down. This is the succession he had traced from Eleazar, who came out of the house of bondage in Egypt, to Jehozadak, who went into the house of bondage in Babylon; and Phinehas stands near its head, the man whose faithfulness won a covenant of an everlasting priesthood for his seed. The witnesses do not divide. Clarke marks the narrowing of the record to Eleazar's family, Keil and Delitzsch the principle of inheritance that makes it a true line of high priests, and Henry the covenant meaning of the descent; and all three read the verse the same way—not as a bare begetting, but as the first firm link in the chain of Israel's chief priests, the office passing from father to son through the family God had marked for it, from the zealous Phinehas onward toward the exile that would one day break the line off. The names dropped from the record—Nadab and Abihu, the whole house of Ithamar—are not forgotten but set aside, that the single thread of the chief priesthood may be followed clean; and even in that narrowing the witnesses hear the historian's purpose, to trace the office and not merely the family, from Eleazar down to the day of Nebuchadnezzar.
8:
A quiet reversal is recorded in this name, and the wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke is the one to point it out. Ahitub begat Zadok; and through this Zadok, he observes, the high priesthood came again into the family of Eleazar. The single clause marks the healing of an old displacement, the office restored to the line from which it had for a season passed.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry had already told the story that Clarke's note presupposes. In the time of the judges the dignity was, upon some occasion, brought into the family of Ithamar, of which Eli was; but in Zadok it returned again to the right line, the elder house of Eleazar. So the name of Zadok, dropped without comment into the middle of the register, carries a whole passage of Israel's history—the fall of Eli's house under judgment and the raising up of a faithful priest to walk before the Lord's anointed for ever.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch, tracing the succession through these generations, read the verse as one more link in the line of high priests they had followed from Eleazar toward the captivity, the office passing from Ahitub to Zadok as from father to heir. The witnesses converge on the significance the bare name hides. Zadok is no common link: with him the priesthood comes home to Eleazar's house, the branch God had first marked for it, after its long sojourn in the line of Ithamar; and Henry and Clarke together let the reader feel the providence in it—that God, through the shifts and failures of the priestly families, kept his purpose and brought the sacred office back at last to the seed of Phinehas, in the very Zadok who would anoint Solomon and found the line that served in the temple until the exile. The one name, dropped without a word of explanation into the middle of the roll, thus holds the whole drama of the priesthood's fall and recovery: Eli's house judged and set aside, and Eleazar's house restored, so that the reader who knows the story hears in “Ahitub begat Zadok” the quiet turning of a long providence. Neither Clarke nor Henry labours the point, but both let it stand—that God governs even the succession of his priests, and that the office lost through one family's sin he gave back through another's faithfulness, keeping his covenant with the seed of Phinehas across the failures of the generations between.
10:
One name in the priestly line is singled out for honour, and the witnesses gather to weigh it. Of Azariah it is said that “he it is that executed the priest's office in the temple that Solomon built,” and the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry takes the words to point at a particular act of courage: this, he supposes, was the Azariah who bravely withstood the presumption of King Uzziah when the king invaded the priest's office, though he risked his neck by so doing (2 Chron. 26:17-18). To have maintained the barriers of the office against so daring an insult was, Henry says, to execute it indeed, and the honour of the phrase is his reward—while Urijah, one of his successors, for his base compliance with Ahaz in building an idolatrous altar, has the disgrace of being left out of the genealogy altogether.

Yet Henry records a rival construction honestly: some think this remark should rather have been added to the grandfather of the same name, the Azariah who was son of Ahimaaz, and that it was he who first officiated in Solomon's temple, so that the note marks not the withstander of Uzziah but the priest who opened the new house's service. The wesleyan-arminian witnesses read the clause of the withstander: Adam Clarke, taking the Johanan just before to be the same as Jehoiada, refers the “executing of the office” to Azariah's dignified opposition to Uzziah, and John Wesley agrees that the words imply he did it worthily, filling his place and valiantly discharging his office in Uzziah's day.

On one point Wesley adds a clarifying note: the temple is called the one “Solomon built” to distinguish it from the second temple, which was building, or already built, when these books were written. The witnesses thus leave the verse with a small unresolved question—whether the honour falls on this Azariah or on his grandfather of the same name—but with one clear sense. To “execute the priest's office” is more than to hold it; it is to serve it faithfully and, at need, to defend it, and Scripture fixes an enduring mark of honour on the priest who did so, as surely as it blots from the roll the priest who betrayed it to a king's idolatry.
14:
