Near the end of the line stands Seraiah, and the wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke fixes his fate from the closing history of the kingdom: this Seraiah was put to death by Nebuchadnezzar (2 Kings 25:18, 25:21), slain among the chief men of Jerusalem when the city fell. The high priest whose name the register reaches almost last did not die in peace at the altar but under the sword of the conqueror.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch, carrying the succession to its close, note that Seraiah begat Jehozadak, and that Jehozadak it was who went into captivity when the Lord carried Judah and Jerusalem away by Nebuchadnezzar's hand—so that the father was killed and the son led into exile, and the priestly line, unbroken for centuries, was at once cut down and carried off. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry had read the whole list toward just this end: the line that began with Eleazar, who came out of Egypt's bondage, ends with Jehozadak, who went into Babylon's, and the generation of Seraiah is the hinge on which that grim symmetry turns.

The witnesses feel the weight of the moment the verse quietly records. Seraiah is no mere name but the last high priest to serve in Solomon's temple before its burning, and his death by Nebuchadnezzar is the visible end of the old order—the office that had run from the wilderness to the monarchy now broken at the altar it served. Clarke's cross-reference to the slaughter at Riblah and Keil and Delitzsch's tracing of the descent to Jehozadak's exile meet in Henry's larger reading: the priesthood could not save the people, nor itself, from the judgment their sins had earned, and the sword that fell on Seraiah showed that the Levitical order made nothing perfect. Yet the line was not utterly extinguished, for Seraiah's son Jehozadak, carried to Babylon, would father the Joshua who came back to build again, so that even in this death the promise was not wholly lost, but only driven underground to rise beyond the exile. The verse that names Seraiah so briefly thus stands, for the witnesses, at the very seam of judgment and hope, the last priest of the old house cut down even as the seed of its restoration is carried away alive.
22:
Among the Kohathites a name of dark memory appears, and the wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke will not let it pass without its story: this is the Korah of the great rebellion, and he sends the reader to the history of the man and his revolt against Moses and Aaron (Num. 16), when the earth opened and swallowed the rebels. That so notorious a figure should stand in the sacred line of the singers is itself a wonder the verse quietly holds.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch, however, find the opening of this Kohathite list perplexing on other grounds. The very beginning—“the sons of Kohath, Amminadab his son, Korah his son, Assir his son”—is strange, they say, for by the law Kohath's second son was Izhar, and it was Izhar who begat Korah (Exod. 6:18-21); whereas here an Amminadab stands at the head, a name found nowhere else as a son of Kohath. They incline to think Amminadab and Izhar one man under two names, so that the descent still runs, as the law has it, through Izhar to Korah and his sons.

The reformed John Gill, tracing this same line down toward Samuel, had already made that identification, taking Amminadab for Izhar and carrying the chain on through Korah and Assir and Elkanah. The witnesses thus read the verse on two levels at once. Clarke fastens on the awful history the name Korah carries, the pattern of presumption against God's appointed order; Keil and Delitzsch and Gill labour over the textual knot at the head of the list, and agree in loosing it by the equation of Amminadab with Izhar. What holds the two readings together is the marvel the verse embodies: that out of the very house of Korah, whose father perished for rising against the priesthood, God raised up the singers of his temple and the prophet Samuel, so that the line of a rebel became, by grace, a line of praise—the sons of Korah lending their name to psalms sung before the very altar their forefather had defied. It is a stroke of mercy the register records without comment, but the witnesses do not miss it: the same house that once rose against Moses stands, generations later, at the head of the temple's song.
28:
A textual wound lies open in this verse, and the witnesses set themselves to bind it. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke calls it a great mistake: the sons of Samuel are here given as “the firstborn Vashni, and Abiah,” whereas the older record names Samuel's firstborn as Joel and his second as Abiah (1 Sam. 8:2). The word Joel, he explains, has fallen out of the text, and the Hebrew for “and the second,” vesheni, has been mistaken for a proper name, Vashni; the Greek and Latin and Chaldee copy the blunder, though the Syriac and Arabic read rightly, and Jarchi labours to save the letter of the text by paraphrase. The two, Joel and Abiah, were the sons who judged so badly that the people, oppressed, demanded a king (1 Sam. 8:1).

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reaches the same verdict by his own road. Vashni is no name but a corruption of the phrase “and the second”; Joel the firstborn has dropped away, and the verse should run “Joel the firstborn, and the second Abiah.” He notes too that “Samuel his son” seems to have fallen from the end of the previous verse, the writer perhaps assuming the connexion too well known to specify, or throwing together independent fragments; and he sets the pedigree of Elkanah beside this one (1 Sam. 1:1), where Elihu answers to Eliab and Tohu to Nahath, the names varying yet not past recognition.

Against these two the reformed John Gill had taken a gentler line, harmonising rather than emending: he read Samuel's firstborn as Vashni, whose name was also Joel, noting that the Syriac and Arabic here read Joel outright. So a real difference of method shows itself—Gill keeping Vashni as a name and equating it with Joel, Clarke and Ellicott striking it out altogether as a misread word with Joel lost from the line. Ellicott closes with a caution that tempers the whole: comparing the ten generations from Judah to Jesse (Ruth 4:18-22) with the twenty here reckoned from Levi, he warns that the Hebrew tables did not always supply every link, but were often content with the principal names—so that gaps and slips in such a list are no discredit, but the ordinary condition of an ancient genealogy honestly handed down.
50:
The historian returns to Aaron's sons a second time, and the wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reads the repetition as a window on the writer's method. A list of these had been given already at the head of the chapter; this is a second, shorter one, and its very doubling is a proof, Clarke says, that the author had several tables before him, from which he borrowed as he judged proper. The same descent could thus be set down twice, once at length to the captivity and once in brief, because more than one document supplied it.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch describe the office this second register serves. It is a catalogue of the high priests running from Eleazar to Ahimaaz the son of Zadok (2 Sam. 15:27), who probably succeeded his father in the high-priesthood in the days of Solomon; and it is cast in the same form as the family-lists of the Levites given earlier, each name linked to the last by “his son.” They judge it drawn from the same source as those lists and set here on purpose, to make the transition from the priests to the register of their cities that follows, since it is the dwelling-places of Aaron's sons that the next verses will number.

The witnesses read the verse, then, as a hinge and a fresh beginning. Clarke's point about the several tables and Keil and Delitzsch's about the shared form and its purpose meet in one picture of the Chronicler at work: a compiler with a sheaf of ancient documents, willing to repeat a descent he had already given if a second source offered it and if the repetition served his design. Here the design is plain—having finished the succession of high priests and the account of the Levites' service, he names Aaron's line once more, only so far as Ahimaaz, in order to reach the men to whom the first cities were allotted, and so to swing the narrative from the persons of the priesthood to the places of its dwelling. The second list is no idle echo of the first, but the bridge by which the chapter passes from genealogy to geography.
60:
The tally of thirteen cities does not add up, and the witnesses agree on the cause. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke notes that though the total is given as thirteen, only eleven are here reckoned, Gibeon and Juttah being wanting and the names of some others changed; and he observes that none of the ancient versions supplies the full count of names, though all alike give the sum as thirteen. The number stands right while the list falls short—a plain sign that two names have slipped from the copy.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott names the missing towns and explains the loss. “Gibeon and her pastures” has dropped out, probably by an oversight born of the likeness in sound and form between Gibeon and the Geba that stands nearby; and Juttah too must be restored, so that the eleven of the present text are brought back to their proper thirteen. Of one variant he offers a gentler account: Alemeth and Almon are each a valid formation, and perhaps represent an older and a younger name of the one place, rather than a copyist's error.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch, who had reckoned the same short count a verse or two before, confirm it: before Bethshemesh the name Juttah has been lost, and before Geba the name Gibeon, so that only eleven cities are named though the sum is rightly given as thirteen. The witnesses do not divide; they read the verse as a clear instance of the wear a copied register suffers, and of the way the truth may be recovered—the total itself, kept faithfully, testifying against the defective list and calling the lost names back. It is a small lesson in the trustworthiness of the record even in its blemishes: the arithmetic the Chronicler preserved corrects the very slip his copyists made, and the parallel in Joshua supplies what the eye had skipped, so that Gibeon and Juttah, dropped for a moment from the page, are known still to belong among the cities of the priests. And Ellicott's gentler reading of Alemeth and Almon adds a needed caution: not every difference between the two records is a scribe's error, for a town may honestly bear two names across the years, so that the witnesses correct where the copy has plainly failed yet do not multiply faults beyond what the evidence requires.
69:
A gap yawns in the text here, and the witnesses set about measuring it. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke notes plainly that two cities are wanting: with Aijalon the parallel names also Eltekeh and Gibethon (Josh. 21:23), which have fallen from the present list. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott spells the loss out fully: Joshua's account gives, out of the tribe of Dan, Eltekeh, Gibbethon, Aijalon, and Gath-rimmon, four cities in all, whereas here the tribe of Dan and two of its towns have dropped away, leaving a clear lacuna between the verses.

Over the reason for the gap Ellicott weighs two views. It has been supposed, he reports, that the Chronicler omits mention of Dan, here and elsewhere, out of a religious prejudice, because the city of Dan was the seat of an illicit worship and its name odious to him. But Ellicott himself thinks it far more likely that such omissions are not to be charged on the Chronicler's piety at all, but on the imperfection of his sources or the carelessness, and perhaps the malpractice, of his copyists and editors. So the question stands open: is the missing tribe a deliberate silence or an accident of transmission?

The witnesses lean, with Ellicott, toward the humbler explanation, and the whole tenor of the chapter supports it. Again and again the grammarians have found the city-list scarred by lost names and mistaken words—Gibeon and Juttah dropped, a whole line fallen out, a plural set wrong—and each time the cause has been the ordinary wear of a copied document, not the design of the author. To read the absence of Dan as pious censorship is to credit the Chronicler with a scruple the text nowhere avows, when the simpler and better-attested cause lies ready to hand. Clarke's flat notice of the two wanting cities and Ellicott's careful preference for corruption over prejudice meet in the same sober conclusion: the register is a true one, handed down through fallible hands, and its silences are more often the copyist's fault than the historian's choice. To lay the loss of Dan at the copyist's door rather than the compiler's is, on their reading, both the kinder and the likelier judgment, and of a piece with all the chapter has shown of a worn but honest text.
71:
Clarke pauses over the whole barren register at this point, and turns the reader's impatience into a lesson. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke grants that several of these cities bear names here different from those in Joshua (Josh. 21:28), and then confesses how the long roll strikes a modern eye: how barren, he says, is this register to us, both of incident and of interest. Yet the confession becomes a defence. As barren rocks and sandy deserts make integral and necessary parts of the globe, so do these genealogical and geographical tables make necessary parts of the history of God's providence and grace, in the maintenance of truth and the establishment of the Church of Christ; and therefore, he urges, no one who fears God will either despise them or lightly esteem them.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch supply the plain matter over which Clarke reflects: this verse gives the Gershonites their cities out of the half-tribe of Manasseh, Golan in Bashan and Ashtaroth, the list answering to the parallel in Joshua. Their steady work of aligning name with name across the two records is itself an answer to the reader who finds the passage dry—showing that behind the dull catalogue lies a real and recoverable allotment, faithfully kept.

The witnesses thus meet on the worth of what seems worthless. Clarke lifts the eye from the tedium of the names to the providence they serve, and Keil and Delitzsch bend to the labour of preserving and correcting them; and between the preacher's reflection and the grammarian's patience the verse is redeemed from mere dryness. Golan and Ashtaroth, given the singers' kinsmen in far Bashan, are two more tokens that the tribe of Levi was planted through the whole land—and Clarke's word stands over them and over all their fellows, that the tables which try our patience are yet a part of the record of God's faithfulness, not to be scorned by any who fear him. The very barrenness of the list, on his reading, is a summons to reverence: God has thought these names worth keeping, and it is not for us to hold cheap what he has been at such pains to preserve.
24:
The Kohathite names begin to double back on themselves, and the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sets the two forms of the line side by side to see whether they are one. In the ascending pedigree of Heman the corresponding names are Tahath, Zephaniah, Azariah, and Joel, where here they run Tahath, Uriel, Uzziah, and Shaul; and it would be easy, he grants, to take Uriel and Zephaniah for two different sons of Tahath, so that the lines diverge. But he marks the tell-tale equivalences: Uzziah may well be the Azariah of the other list, as the king bore both names at once, and the correspondence between the two registers is so close throughout that he can hardly avoid concluding them substantially the same, Uriel known also as Zephaniah and Shaul as Joel.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch had reached the same seam from the other direction, treating this string—Elkanah, Ebiasaph, Assir, Tahath, Uriel, Uzziah, Shaul—as one of the three fragments of the Kohathite descent, and comparing it link by link with Heman's ascending line to find where the two answer to each other. Both grammarians, working the same ground, incline to identity rather than divergence, taking the differing names for variants of one man rather than tokens of two.

The witnesses thus read the verse as another instance of the register's doubled and shifting forms, and of the patient method by which such doublings are resolved. The house of Kohath is given twice over, once counted downward and once upward, and where a name changes between the two the commentators do not multiply persons but weigh whether a single man stood under two names, as so often in these lists. Ellicott's careful equations—Uzziah with Azariah, Uriel with Zephaniah, Shaul with Joel—and Keil and Delitzsch's matching of the fragments serve the one conviction that has governed the whole chapter: beneath the variant spellings runs a single true descent, and the very repetitions that seem at first to confuse the line are, rightly read, the double witness that confirms it. It is the labour of comparison, not the abundance of names, that yields the truth here, and the witnesses spend it freely, unwilling to let a change of spelling break a descent that the parallel shows to be whole.
25:
The sons of Elkanah—Amasai and Ahimoth—open a fresh branch, and the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reads it not as a new family but as the same line under another aspect. It is natural, he says, to identify this Elkanah with the Elkanah named in Heman's ascending pedigree, for the posterity of the two are so nearly alike; only if the descents had differed would one need to take him for the earlier Elkanah mentioned among the sons of Kohath. The near-identity of the offspring settles the matter: one Elkanah, one line, given here downward and there upward.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch had opened the same question at the head of this fragment, noting that with “the sons of Elkanah, Amasai and Ahimoth” a new list appears to begin, and reasoning that a comparison with Heman's genealogy—where Elkanah, Amasai, and Mahath stand in order—suggests this Elkanah to be the son of the Joel there named, and Ahimoth without difficulty another form of Mahath. The two grammarians thus converge: the seeming new start is the old line resumed, its names only re-spelled.

The witnesses read the verse, then, as one more knot in the Kohathite thread and one more patient loosing of it. The historian, drawing on more than one document, sets down the sons of Elkanah as though beginning afresh, and the copies vary the forms—Ahimoth here, Mahath there—so that an incautious eye might count two families where there is one. Ellicott's appeal to the likeness of the posterity and Keil and Delitzsch's to the order of the names in Heman's line meet in the same judgment, that this Elkanah and his sons stand in the single descent that runs toward Samuel and the singer sprung from him. The lesson is the chapter's constant one: where the register seems to branch, the witnesses look to the parallel and find the branches grown from one stem, the variant names no proof of division but the ordinary dress of an ancient pedigree kept in more shapes than one. And the stakes are not small, for it is Samuel's own house that hangs on these links; the grammarians take pains to keep it whole because through this Elkanah the prophet's line, and the singer Heman's after it, is carried back without a break to Kohath and to Levi.
38:
Heman's line reaches its source in this verse—“the son of Izhar, the son of Kohath, the son of Levi, the son of Israel”—and the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott pauses on that final clause. Asaph and Ethan, he notes, are traced only to Levi, and it was not needful to repeat “son of Israel” in each case; the one full ascent, carried up to the patriarch himself, stands for all three, anchoring the whole company of singers in the father of the nation. For the names between, he refers the reader back to his notes on the earlier Kohathite list, the two lines being identical—though their numerous variants, he adds, seem to imply that the author drew from different documents.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch had carried the same ascent to its head, following Heman's pedigree up through Ebiasaph and Korah and Izhar to Kohath, to Levi, and to Israel, and marking that this line runs one step further than Asaph's and Ethan's, which halt at Levi. The reaching of the great name at the end is deliberate: it binds the chief musician to the covenant stock, and shows him no stranger drafted to the work but a true son of the chosen people.

The witnesses close Heman's genealogy on this note of rootedness. The list does not stop at the tribe but climbs to Israel himself, and in that final word the singer at the centre of the temple choir is joined to the whole history of the covenant: he leads the praise as a son of Israel, in a descent as old as the nation's father. Ellicott's observation that the one full ascent serves for all three masters, and Keil and Delitzsch's tracing of it to the patriarch, meet in the same sense—that the many variant names along the way take nothing from the certainty of the descent, but rather, as Ellicott says, betray the several ancient documents from which it was gathered, each preserving the line in its own spelling, all agreeing that Heman sprang from Levi the son of Israel. The song of the sanctuary is thus shown to rise from the very root of the people it was appointed to serve.
57:
The priests' portion in Judah is set down, and the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott begins by mending the wording. The Hebrew, he notes, reads “the cities of refuge, Hebron and Libnah,” yet of all the towns here named only Hebron was an asylum for the manslayer; the other cities of refuge lay elsewhere—Kedesh in Naphtali, Shechem, Bezer, Ramoth in Gilead, and Golan (Josh. 20:7-8)—so that the plural here misleads, and our translators have rightly followed the Hebrew margin in reading “the cities of Judah.” The same inaccuracy, he warns, will recur further down. He notes too that after Jattir the usual clause “with her suburbs” has fallen away, the pastures dropping from the copy.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch had made the same corrections in their survey of the priestly cities, marking that the plural “cities of refuge” is incorrect since Hebron alone was such, that Joshua rightly has the singular, and that the suburb-clause is missing after Jattir. The two grammarians move in step, reading the verse against the parallel and restoring what the transcription has blurred.

The witnesses thus read the verse as another panel of the priestly allotment marred by the same small flaw—the loose plural of “cities of refuge” fastened onto a list where only one such city stands. What matters to them is less the slip than the method of its cure: the true reading is recovered not by guess but by the witness of Joshua and the sober logic of the institution itself, since it was known and fixed which six towns were the asylums of the land, and Hebron the only one among these. Ellicott's care to name the other five refuges and Keil and Delitzsch's flat correction of the plural serve the same end, to keep the record clear: Hebron heads the priests' cities both as the chief of them and as the one refuge among them, and the pastures that the copy dropped after Jattir belonged, as every Levitical town's did, to the gift. The grammarians' insistence that only Hebron was a refuge is no pedantry, for it guards the institution's own order: the six asylums of the land were fixed and known, and a careless plural must not be allowed to blur so weighty a distinction.
59:
Two names with a shared meaning meet in this verse, and the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott draws out the play between them. The Ashan here named appears in Joshua as Ain: the one word means “smoke,” the other “fountain,” and the place, he suggests, may have borne both names together, from a spring that rose like a rising column of smoke. He marks besides that “Juttah and her pastures” has fallen out of the list here, as the parallel shows (Josh. 21:16), and that Joshua closes the reckoning with a sum this text omits—nine cities out of the two tribes of Judah and Simeon.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch had noted the same variant, taking Ain to be the more correct form against the Ashan of the present copy, and counting Juttah among the names lost before Bethshemesh, so that the tally falls short of its own total. The two grammarians agree on both points—the shifted name and the dropped town—and read the one as an honest variation, the other as a copyist's omission to be supplied from Joshua.

The witnesses find in the verse a small but telling picture of how such a register lives and wears. A single town may answer to two names, each true, catching a different feature of the place—its fountain and the smoke-like plume of its spring—so that Ashan and Ain are not a contradiction but a double memory; and at the same stroke a real name, Juttah, may simply drop from a weary copy and have to be called back from the parallel. Ellicott's fancy of the smoking fountain and Keil and Delitzsch's plain arithmetic serve one reading: that the Chronicler handed down a genuine and ancient list, rich enough to hold a town's two names and human enough to lose one here and there, its truth recoverable at every point by the witness of the older book. The cities of the priests in Judah and Simeon are thus numbered, name by name, even where the copy stumbles, that the record of Levi's dwelling might stand complete. The smoking fountain and the missing town, set side by side, catch in miniature the whole character of the register: ancient, living, worn in places, yet everywhere recoverable, and everywhere worth the recovering.
61:
The remaining Kohathites' allotment is stated here, and the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott finds the text again mutilated. A comparison with Joshua shows what it should say: that to the sons of Kohath which were left, out of the clans of the tribe of Ephraim, and out of the tribe of Dan, and out of the half-tribe of Manasseh, ten cities were given by lot (Josh. 21:5). But the present text is strangely redundant—“out of the half of the tribe of the half of Manasseh,” a phrase found nowhere else—and Ellicott takes the tangle for a bad emendation of some corrupt reading, the names of Ephraim and Dan having dropped away and a doubled Manasseh left standing in their place. The passage from Joshua, he judges, undoubtedly gives the meaning.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch had marked the same gap, noting a hiatus in the verse where the words “Ephraim, and of the tribe of Dan, and” have been omitted, so that the ten cities are left without the tribes they came from, and the sense must be filled out from the parallel. Both grammarians read the corruption the same way, and both repair it by the older record.

The witnesses thus treat the verse as one of the clearest cases in the chapter of a text worn through by copying, and of the discipline that restores it. Ephraim and Dan have fallen from the list of tribes, and the surviving “half of Manasseh” has been doubled into nonsense—yet the number ten stands firm, and Joshua preserves the whole. Ellicott's eye for the impossible phrase and Keil and Delitzsch's for the missing names meet in the one method that has served throughout: to mark the wound honestly, to supply the loss not by conjecture but from the parallel Scripture, and so to recover the true grant—ten cities to the Kohathites who were not of Aaron's line, out of Ephraim and Dan and the half of Manasseh. The very brokenness of the copy, patiently mended, becomes one more proof of the fidelity with which the register was handed on, warts and gaps and all.
63:
The Merarite portion is stated plainly, and the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott has only a small textual note to add: this verse, he observes, stands word for word the same as its counterpart in Joshua (Josh. 21:7), save that it omits the single term “by lot.” The near-exact agreement is itself instructive, showing how closely the Chronicler's register runs with the older record even where, elsewhere, his copy has slipped.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch supply the substance the verse carries: the sons of Merari received twelve cities, by lot, out of the tribes of Reuben, Gad, and Zebulun—the trans-Jordan tribes of Reuben and Gad joined with Zebulun in the north—so that this youngest house of Levi was settled at the eastern and northern edges of the land. The lot, they note, governs the distribution here as throughout, the allotment being no human contrivance but referred to the disposing hand of God.

The witnesses read the verse as a quiet witness both to the honesty of the text and to the providence in the allotment. Where so much of the city-list has shown the marks of wear—dropped names, mistaken plurals, doubled words—here the notice stands whole and almost identical with Joshua, a reminder that the corruption elsewhere is the exception and not the rule, and that the Chronicler's source was a faithful transcript of the ancient record. And the twelve cities given Merari by lot out of Reuben and Gad and Zebulun round out the even-handed provision the chapter has traced: each of Levi's three houses, Gershom and Kohath and Merari, settled by the same divine lot among the tribes, that the landless sons of Levi might have their dwellings, and the tribes of Israel their ministers, from the Jordan's eastern bank to the hills of Zebulun. The plainness of the verse is its own testimony—the record, here at least, kept clean. Set beside the mangled verses on either side, this untroubled agreement with Joshua quietly vindicates the whole: where the copy was spared, it matches the older book almost to the letter, so that the reader may trust that the slips elsewhere are the fault of transmission and not of the allotment the historian faithfully received.
66:
The Kohathite remnant in Ephraim is named again, and the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott judges the received wording barely intelligible. “The residue of the families,” as it stands, can hardly mean what the passage requires; Joshua shows the true sense (Josh. 21:20), and the original must have run, “and to the families of the sons of Kohath—and the cities of their border were of the tribe of Ephraim.” The construction breaks off, he notes, and a fresh start is made with “and the cities,” the whole notice abridged from the fuller account in the older book.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley adds a gloss on the one obscure word: the “coasts” of the verse are better read “borders,” the country of the families contained within its bounds—the territory, that is, that fell to them within the tribe of Ephraim. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch had likewise marked that the cities of the remaining Kohathites lay in Ephraim, the notice answering to Joshua and abridged from it, so that all three witnesses read the verse against the same parallel.

The witnesses thus treat the verse as another instance of the Chronicler's compression and of his copy's wear, met once more by the light of Joshua. The sentence stumbles, its opening phrase strained and its grammar broken, because the historian has abridged a fuller record and a copyist has further blurred it; but the meaning is not lost, for the parallel supplies it, and Wesley's small correction of “coasts” to “borders” clears the last obscurity. What the verse says, rightly read, is simple enough—that the Kohathites who remained received their cities within the borders of Ephraim—and the labour the witnesses spend on its tangled words is of a piece with their whole handling of the chapter: to receive the text as it stands, note where it has suffered, and restore its sense from the surer record, that even the roughest verse of the allotment may yield its true and modest meaning. The stumble in the grammar, far from unsettling the record, only shows the historian abridging an older and fuller notice, and the witnesses trusting the parallel to steady what his shorthand and his copyists had left unclear.
68:
Among the Ephraimite cities one name invites correction and another a memory. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott takes up the first: where this text reads Jokmeam, Joshua has Kibzaim, a name the Greek of the Vatican copy omits; and Ellicott judges Jokmeam probably the right form, the other easily arising from it through the confusion of similar letters, though the site of the town is now unknown. The four cities of these two verses, he notes, all lay in Ephraim.

Then he lets the map speak. Beth-horon, and Gibeon, and Aijalon—towns that stand among these Levitical cities—were the very scenes of that great and providentially determined overthrow of the five kings of the Amorites, when the sun stood still and the hailstones fell (Josh. 10); and it was fitting, Ellicott feels, that such fields of God's mighty acts should be assigned to the sacred tribe, the ground of the Lord's victories given to the Lord's ministers. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch had listed the same towns—Jokmeam and Bethhoron among the cities of the remaining Kohathites—reading them, as ever, against the parallel in Joshua.

The witnesses thus find in the verse both a textual nicety and a stroke of holy fitness. The question of Jokmeam or Kibzaim is settled, as so often, by weighing which form could breed the other, and Ellicott decides for Jokmeam; but the deeper interest lies in the places themselves. That Bethhoron and Aijalon, where Joshua's long day was won and the Amorite host broken, should become dwellings of the Levites is no accident to Ellicott's eye but a quiet propriety—the tribe that served the altar planted on the very ground where God had fought for Israel, so that the memory of the great deliverance was kept in the towns of those whose office was to teach it. The dry list, read so, becomes a map of grace, the cities of the ministers laid down upon the fields of the Lord's own victories. Ellicott's decision for Jokmeam and his reflection on the Amorite battlefields go together: the same care that weighs a letter to recover a name lifts its eye, when the names are sure, to the providence written across the map, so that textual patience and holy wonder meet in the reading of a single verse.
70:
Two names that ought to be places turn out, on inspection, to be men, and the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott untangles them. Where this text gives Aner and Bileam among the cities of the half-tribe of Manasseh, Joshua reads Taanach and Gath-rimmon (Josh. 21:25)—and the latter, Gath-rimmon, is plainly a mere repetition from the verse before. Bileam, he shows, is but the Hebrew spelling of Balaam, and should rather be Ibleam (Josh. 17:11); while Aner is elsewhere the name of a man, one of Abraham's allies (Gen. 14:13), and here should be Taanach, whose Hebrew letters closely resemble it.

Ellicott corrects the wording too: the clause “for the family of the remnant of the sons of Kohath” is better read “unto the family of the sons of Kohath who were left,” a kind of subscription to the whole list of these cities, and the singular “family” should be the plural, as Joshua has it. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch had set down the same two towns, Aner and Bileam, out of the half-tribe for the remnant of Kohath, reading them, like Ellicott, against the parallel record.

The witnesses read the verse as a knot of transcription patiently combed out. Two proper names of men—Aner the ally of Abraham, Balaam the soothsayer—have crept into the place of two towns, Taanach and Ibleam, through the likeness of their letters and the stumbling of a copyist, and a third name has been repeated by mistake from the verse above. Ellicott's eye for the shapes of the Hebrew words restores the true reading, and Keil and Delitzsch's steady appeal to Joshua confirms it; and once more the method proves its worth, that where the copy has blundered the parallel Scripture sets it right. The two cities given the last of the Kohathites in Manasseh are recovered from the corruption that had all but buried them, and the register carried faithfully to its close—the truth of the allotment holding firm beneath even the oddest slips of the pen. That two men's names could stand for two towns, and be set right again by the shape of the Hebrew and the witness of Joshua, is the chapter's whole discipline in little: receive the text, mark its wounds, and heal them from the surer record.
11:
Midway through the high-priestly roll the same names come round again—Azariah begets Amariah, and Amariah begets Ahitub—and the witnesses read the verse as one more link in the long chain of succession they had taken up together at its head. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch, who count the whole descent of Eleazar through two-and-twenty generations, treat this pair as they treat the rest: the word “he begat” repeated at each name, and the son so begotten, living on after his father, inheriting the high-priestly dignity. There is no fresh comment to make on Amariah or Ahitub in themselves; they are two more of the priests through whom the office passed from the wilderness toward the captivity.

Yet the reader may notice, as the grammarians' careful tally invites him to, that these very names have been heard before in the list, an Amariah and an Ahitub standing higher up as well, so that the line seems for a moment to repeat itself. Keil and Delitzsch's insistence that the enumeration is a genuine succession, drawn by the older commentators from the reiterated “begat,” meets just such a difficulty: the recurrence of a name is no proof of confusion, for the same honoured names ran again and again in the priestly house, father naming son for grandfather, generation after generation.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry's reading of the whole line governs this verse as much as any. The descent, he had said, runs from Eleazar, who came out of Egypt's bondage, to Jehozadak, who went into Babylon's, and not every name in it belonged to a man who served with honour; some, like Urijah who built an idolatrous altar for Ahaz, were struck from the roll for their sins, and perhaps others silently passed over. Amariah and Ahitub stand, then, among the priests whom the record keeps—neither singled out for praise like the Azariah who withstood a king, nor blotted out like the Urijah who served one's idolatry, but simply carried in the succession, the office moving through them as through the rest. The witnesses leave the verse as what it is: a quiet middle link, kept because the chain must be whole, in a line that could not save its people from exile and so pointed beyond itself to a priesthood that could.
35:
Zuph and Elkanah and Mahath and Amasai—the names climb on in Heman's ascending pedigree, and the witnesses read them as the very stretch over which they had already laboured to prove the two Kohathite lists one. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch, carrying Heman's descent upward from the singer toward Levi, pass through just these links, and their governing point holds here: this ascending line and the descending list given earlier are, for all their variant forms, the same house set out twice.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott had fastened on this portion in particular. Zophai, he showed, stands to the Zuph here named as Chelubai to Chelub, a fuller by-form of one word; and if the Mahath of this verse be the same as the Ahimoth of the descending list, then the two series coincide exactly—“Amasai his son, Ahimoth his son, Elkanah his son, Zophai his son”—the very names of this ascent answering, step for step, to those counted downward before. It was to reach this agreement that Ellicott struck out the doubled Elkanah of the earlier verse as a copyist's intrusion.

The witnesses thus find in this bare rung of the ladder a quiet confirmation of all their patient comparison. Zuph and Elkanah and Mahath and Amasai are not new men to be described but known links, the same that had appeared under slightly other shapes in the downward list, and their reappearance here in the ascent is the double witness that the line is one and true. Keil and Delitzsch's steady climb and Ellicott's matching of by-form to by-form meet in the single conviction the chapter has pressed throughout: that beneath the shifting spellings of these registers runs a genuine descent, and that Heman the singer is anchored, through Zuph and Amasai and their fathers, in the house of Kohath the son of Levi. The verse adds no incident, but it tightens the knot of proof, showing the same names holding firm whether the historian counts them from the singer up or from the patriarch down. And what is secured is no small thing, for this is the pedigree of Samuel's house carried on behind its singer, so that the grammarians' labour over a by-form of Zuph or a doubled Elkanah serves in the end to anchor the prophet's line, and the temple's chief musician sprung from it, past all dispute in Levi.
41:
Ethni and Zerah and Adaiah carry Asaph's line another stage toward its root, and the witnesses read the names as part of the ascent they had already weighed against the shorter Gershonite list. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch, tracing Asaph's pedigree upward through Zimmah and Shimei and Jahath to Gershom and Levi, pass through these very links on the way, and count the whole line the descent of the Gershonite singer from the younger son Shimei.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott had drawn the sharpest observation from just these names. Three of them—Jahath, Zimmah, and Zerah—stand in the same order of descent from Gershom as in the brief Gershonite list given earlier; and here the Adaiah of Asaph's line answers to the Iddo of that list, while the Ethni is in Hebrew not unlike its Jeaterai, so that the two registers, the short and the long, are seen to overlap, Asaph's pedigree having been in part anticipated already. The correspondence, he judged, was too close and too orderly to be chance.

The witnesses thus treat the verse as another proof of the register's hidden unity. Ethni and Zerah and Adaiah are not fresh figures but the recurring links by which the fuller pedigree of the singer is bound to the shorter notice of his house, the same men under names a little shifted—Adaiah for Iddo, Ethni for Jeaterai—so that what looked like two separate lists prove to be one descent told twice. Keil and Delitzsch's tracing of the ascent to Gershom and Ellicott's matching of the recurring names serve the conviction that has run through the whole chapter: that beneath the variant spellings of these Levite genealogies lies a single true line, and that Asaph, like Heman before him, is shown a genuine son of Levi, his place at the right hand of the choir warranted by a descent that the historian, drawing on more than one document, has preserved in more than one form. The bare names, patiently compared, become the quiet guarantee of the singer's Levitical right, so that when Asaph lifts his voice at Heman's side it is as a true son of Gershom and of Levi, and not as a stranger borrowed for the work.
47:
Ethan's ascent ends at Levi, and with it the last of the three singers' pedigrees is brought home. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch, tracing the Merarite line upward through Mahli and Mushi to Merari and Levi, close here the shortest of the three genealogies, and pass at once to the note that follows, that the other Levites, the brethren of these singers, were given for every service of the house of God. The verse is the foot of the ladder set at the beginning of Ethan's descent, now reaching the tribe's own father.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry had marked this very reaching: Ethan, like Heman before him, has his pedigree traced up here as high as Levi, so that the Merarite singer is shown as truly of the sacred tribe as his fellows at the centre and the right hand. And the wesleyan-arminian John Wesley's word hangs over the ascent, that this Ethan is the Jeduthun of the later registers and the psalm-titles, so that the bare climb of Merarite names belongs to a man remembered among the chief musicians of Israel, whose sons served with harp and cymbal in the house of the Lord.

The witnesses read the verse, then, as the completing of a deliberate symmetry. Three times the historian has led a singer's line upward to its root—Heman the Kohathite to Israel himself, Asaph the Gershonite to Levi, and Ethan the Merarite here to Levi again—so that each of the three houses of the tribe is shown to have its master singer, and each master singer his warrant in the covenant descent. Keil and Delitzsch's tracing of the shortest line to Merari and Levi rounds out the pattern, and their turning at once to the service of the other Levites sets the singers in their place among the whole company of the tribe. Henry's note that Ethan too reaches Levi and Wesley's that Ethan is Jeduthun meet in the point the arrangement was built to make: that the praise of God's house rose from all Levi, ordered and rooted, no branch without its voice in the choir and none without its name in the pedigree, the youngest house of Merari joined through Ethan to the song as fully as the eldest.
53:
The second priestly list ends at Ahimaaz, and the witnesses read the closing names—Zadok his son, Ahimaaz his son—as the point to which this shorter register was all along tending. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch had described this catalogue of the high priests as running from Eleazar down to Ahimaaz the son of Zadok (2 Sam. 15:27), who probably succeeded his father in the high-priesthood in the days of Solomon; and it is with him that the list stops, for it is his father's house whose dwelling-places the following verses will number.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke had explained, at the head of this second roll, why it should end so much sooner than the first. This is a briefer list than the great one given earlier, and its very existence, less extensive than the other, is a proof that the writer had several tables before him, from which he borrowed as he judged proper. The shorter register reaches only to Ahimaaz because its purpose was not to trace the priesthood to the captivity, as the first had done, but to bring the descent down just far enough to reach the men to whom the Levitical cities were given.

The witnesses thus read the verse as a hinge rather than a full stop. Ahimaaz, the son of that Zadok whom David advanced—the same Ahimaaz who had run with tidings from the field of Absalom's defeat—stands here as the last of the priests the list means to name, and his appearance turns the narrative from the persons of the priesthood to the places of its dwelling. Keil and Delitzsch's note that he succeeded his father in Solomon's day and Clarke's that the whole shorter roll bespeaks a compiler working from more than one document meet in the same sense: this second list is no rival to the first but a stepping-stone, laid down to carry the reader from the high-priestly succession to the register of the Levites' cities, and stopping at Ahimaaz precisely because it is his father's sons whose towns the next verses will begin to count. The line of priests, told once to the exile and once to Ahimaaz, hands the story on to the geography of the tribe.
77:
The last of the Merarite cities in Zebulun brings the northern portion of the list to its close, and the grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch mark both the grant and its defect. To the sons of Merari, the remaining Levites still to be numbered, were given out of the tribe of Zebulun the cities of Rimmon and Tabor; but only two are here named where the parallel in Joshua has four (Josh. 21:34-37), two having dropped from the present text. The phrase they render “to the sons of Merari the remaining” they take to mark these off as the last of the Levites whose portion is yet to be told.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke's reflection upon the whole of this dry ground bears directly on such a verse. How barren, he had granted, is this register to us, both of incident and of interest; and yet, as barren rocks and sandy deserts make integral and necessary parts of the globe, so do these genealogical and geographical tables make necessary parts of the history of God's providence and grace, in the maintenance of truth and the building of the Church of Christ, so that no one who fears God will despise or lightly esteem them. Two towns of Zebulun, with two more silently lost from the copy, are just the kind of notice that tries a reader's patience and yet, on Clarke's showing, deserves his reverence.

The witnesses thus read the verse as one more sober step toward the completion of the whole allotment. The defect—two cities named for four—is of a piece with the wear the grammarians have traced all through the register, and it takes nothing from the substance, for the parallel supplies the loss and the Merarites are seen, here as elsewhere, provided for out of the tribes among whom they were scattered. Keil and Delitzsch's plain notice of the missing towns and Clarke's defence of the worth of the whole meet in the reading that has governed the chapter's close: that even the barest verse of the catalogue is part of the record of God's faithfulness to his landless tribe, and that the sons of Merari, gathered up here among the last to be numbered, had their dwellings appointed them as surely as their brethren, that no house of Levi should want a place in Israel.
